subUnits.title: 7.1 Isomerism in Hydrocarbons
notes: - The phenomenon where two or more compounds have the same molecular formula but different structural formulas and physical/chemical properties.
a) Chain Isomerism: Different arrangements of the carbon skeleton (e.g., straight chain vs. branched chain).
b) Position Isomerism: Different positions of the functional group or multiple bond on the carbon chain.
c) Functional Isomerism: Different functional groups entirely (more common in oxygenated organics, e.g., ethanol and dimethyl ether).
- Explains why there are millions of organic compounds despite only a few elements.
- Isomers can have vastly different boiling points, densities, and reactivities.
- In the Nigerian petroleum industry, branched-chain isomers (like isooctane) are highly valued because they burn more smoothly in car engines, reducing 'knocking' compared to straight-chain isomers.
diagramDescriptions: Diagram showing chain vs position isomerism
subUnits.youtubeQuery: SS1 Chemistry isomerism chain position functional hydrocarbons site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Isomers are compounds that have:
opts: Different molecular formulas but same structure
Same molecular formula but different structures
quiz.explanation: Isomers share the same molecular formula but differ in their structural arrangement.

quiz.q: Butane and 2-methylpropane are examples of:
quiz.explanation: They have the same formula (C₄H₁₀) but different carbon skeleton arrangements (straight vs. branched).

quiz.q: Position isomerism occurs when there is a difference in:
opts: The position of a functional group or double bond
quiz.explanation: Position isomers have the same carbon skeleton but the functional group is attached at different positions.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a characteristic of isomers?
opts: They cannot be separated by physical means
quiz.explanation: Despite having the same molecular formula, isomers often have different physical properties like boiling point and density.

quiz.q: The phenomenon of isomerism explains why:
opts: There are very few organic compounds
Carbon can form millions of different compounds
quiz.explanation: Isomerism, along with catenation, allows carbon to form a vast number of unique compounds.

quiz.q: In the Nigerian petroleum industry, branched-chain isomers are preferred for petrol because they:
quiz.explanation: Branched isomers burn more smoothly, increasing the octane rating and reducing engine knocking.

quiz.q: How many structural isomers does pentane (C₅H₁₂) have?
quiz.explanation: Pentane has 3 isomers: pentane, 2-methylbutane, and 2,2-dimethylpropane.

quiz.q: Functional isomerism involves compounds with:
opts: Different functional groups but same molecular formula
quiz.explanation: Functional isomers have the same molecular formula but belong to different homologous series (e.g., alcohol and ether).

quiz.q: Which pair represents position isomers?
quiz.explanation: But-1-ene and But-2-ene have the double bond at different positions on the same 4-carbon chain.

quiz.q: Isomerism is NOT exhibited by:
quiz.explanation: Methane (C1), Ethane (C2), and Propane (C3) are too small to have different structural arrangements.

subUnits.title: 7.2 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons (Alkenes and Alkynes)
notes: - Hydrocarbons containing at least one carbon-carbon double bond (C=C).
- Properties: More reactive than alkanes due to the presence of the double bond.
- Hydrocarbons containing at least one carbon-carbon triple bond (C≡C).
- Properties: Highly reactive; ethyne (acetylene) burns with a very hot flame used in welding.
- Bromine Water Test: Unsaturated hydrocarbons decolorize orange/brown bromine water rapidly (addition reaction).
- Acidified KMnO₄ Test: Unsaturated hydrocarbons decolorize purple potassium manganate(VII) solution.
- Ethene is a key raw material in the Nigerian petrochemical industry for producing polyethylene (plastics).
- Ethyne (acetylene) is widely used by Nigerian artisans and welders for cutting and joining metals.
diagramDescriptions: Diagram of the bromine water test for unsaturation
subUnits.youtubeQuery: SS1 Chemistry unsaturated hydrocarbons alkenes alkynes bromine water test site:youtube.com

quiz.q: The general formula for alkenes is:
quiz.explanation: Alkenes contain one double bond, giving them the general formula CₙH₂ₙ.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an alkyne?
quiz.explanation: Ethyne (C₂H₂) contains a carbon-carbon triple bond, making it an alkyne.

quiz.q: Unsaturated hydrocarbons are more reactive than alkanes because they contain:
quiz.explanation: The pi bonds in double and triple bonds are weaker and more susceptible to addition reactions.

quiz.q: The test for unsaturation using bromine water results in:
quiz.explanation: Unsaturated compounds undergo an addition reaction with bromine, decolorizing the orange/brown solution.

quiz.q: Ethyne is commonly known as:
quiz.explanation: Ethyne is widely known by its common name, acetylene.

quiz.q: In Nigeria, ethyne is primarily used by artisans for:
quiz.explanation: Ethyne burns with a very hot flame, making it ideal for oxy-acetylene welding.

quiz.q: The first member of the alkene homologous series is:
quiz.explanation: Methene cannot exist because a double bond requires at least two carbon atoms. Ethene (C₂H₄) is the first.

quiz.q: When ethene reacts with hydrogen in the presence of a nickel catalyst, the product is:
quiz.explanation: This is a hydrogenation (addition) reaction: C₂H₄ + H₂ → C₂H₆ (ethane).

quiz.q: Acidified potassium manganate(VII) (KMnO₄) is used to test for unsaturation. A positive test is indicated by:
opts: The solution turning from purple to colorless
The solution turning from colorless to purple
quiz.explanation: Unsaturated compounds reduce the purple MnO₄⁻ ion to colorless Mn²⁺.

quiz.q: Which of the following compounds will NOT decolorize bromine water?
quiz.explanation: Ethane is a saturated alkane and does not undergo addition reactions with bromine water under normal conditions.

subUnits.title: 7.3 Aromatic Hydrocarbons (Benzene)
notes: - Aromatic hydrocarbons (arenes) contain at least one benzene ring.
- Structure: A planar, hexagonal ring of 6 carbon atoms with delocalized pi electrons (often represented as a hexagon with a circle inside).
- Physical: Colorless, volatile liquid with a distinctive sweet odour. Insoluble in water but soluble in organic solvents.
- Chemical: Undergoes substitution reactions rather than addition reactions (unlike alkenes) due to the stability of the delocalized electron ring.
- Sources: Obtained from the fractional distillation of coal tar and catalytic reforming of petroleum.
- Uses: Starting material for manufacturing plastics (polystyrene), synthetic fibers (nylon), detergents, dyes, and pharmaceuticals.
- Benzene is highly toxic and carcinogenic (causes cancer, particularly leukemia).
- Strict safety protocols are required when handling it in laboratories and industries.
- Benzene and its derivatives (like toluene and xylene) are important products of the Nigerian petrochemical sector.
- Awareness of benzene's toxicity is crucial in Nigerian industrial settings and in the regulation of fuel quality (reducing benzene content in petrol).
diagramDescriptions: Hexagonal structure of benzene with delocalized electrons
Uses of benzene derivatives in industry
subUnits.youtubeQuery: SS1 Chemistry aromatic hydrocarbons benzene structure properties uses site:youtube.com

quiz.q: The molecular formula of benzene is:
quiz.explanation: Benzene has the formula C₆H₆, representing a highly unsaturated but stable ring structure.

quiz.q: The structure of benzene is best described as:
opts: A straight chain with triple bonds
A planar hexagonal ring with delocalized pi electrons
A branched chain with double bonds
quiz.explanation: Benzene is a flat, hexagonal ring where electrons are shared equally among all six carbon atoms.

quiz.q: Unlike alkenes, benzene primarily undergoes:
quiz.explanation: The stable delocalized electron ring makes benzene prefer substitution over addition to maintain its aromaticity.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a major industrial use of benzene?
opts: As a starting material for plastics and synthetic fibers
quiz.explanation: Benzene is a key precursor in the synthesis of polystyrene, nylon, and various dyes.

quiz.q: Benzene is obtained commercially from:
opts: Fractional distillation of coal tar and reforming of petroleum
quiz.explanation: Coal tar and catalytic reforming of petroleum are the primary sources of aromatic hydrocarbons.

quiz.q: A major health hazard associated with benzene exposure is that it is:
quiz.explanation: Benzene is a known carcinogen, linked to blood cancers like leukemia.

quiz.q: Benzene is soluble in:
quiz.explanation: As a non-polar organic compound, benzene dissolves in non-polar organic solvents but not in polar water.

quiz.q: The distinctive odour of benzene is often described as:
quiz.explanation: Benzene has a characteristic sweet, aromatic odour.

quiz.q: In the Nigerian petroleum industry, reducing benzene content in petrol is important to:
opts: Reduce toxic emissions and health risks
quiz.explanation: Lowering benzene content improves fuel safety and reduces environmental and health hazards.

quiz.q: The stability of the benzene ring is due to:
quiz.explanation: The delocalized pi electrons create a stable, resonance-stabilized aromatic system.
